The last time our expressways were safe was starting in December of 1985 when the ISP took over patrol responsibilities from the Chicago Police Department. ISP had manpower from around the state temporarily working the Chicago area expressways and there were troopers everywhere. Mike Royko, the legendary Chicago newspaper columnist, even wrote an article about the impressive change once ISP took over and how everyone was pretty much obeying the law or being pulled over. Over the coming years, ISP manpower started slowly diminishing and now, once again, there are only a few Troopers assigned to the Chicago expressways each shift. This is poor leadership from the governors office.

Not all that complicated,  lights siren's we change siren pitch at intersections we do slow down and yeld while we look and listen, also accross the country their are opticoms that control traffic, blocks ahead signaled from the emergency responding vehicles aids and chaging traffic flow. It  also sends a response signal when other emergency vehicles are approaching the intersection.  when we know of multi units heading to the same scene we radio ahead our cross intersectional approach a heads up to the other units (dispatch hates when we do that ) All of this on top of the on going driver training we do every year. Alote goes into it, we just dont go flying blind at high speeds into intetsections. But yes we all are human and unfortunately mistakes happen and adrenaline is hard to control from time to time. Not making accuses.  We really try hard not to F@#$ up. Its a fine line getting to the scene in time to help and save a life , also advoiding bad drivers and dear in the headlight drivers, knowing and map checking your address ear marking your water supply and the best tactics for the scene where heading to, plus weaving thru traffic because know one pulls over anymore, We kinda got to be part race car driver part old lady driver, part glass delivery driver,  part owl with your head on a constant swivel, if your head isnt swiveling something is wrong. So the first 10-15 min. of every fire call is hell for the Fire Engineer/ or Ladder truck operator. So we dont set out to wreck thats just all kinds of bad , and that movie clip is B.S. 90% of the time we deeply care about the other crews when smothing goes wrong, their are some bad eggs in every part of life probably 10% or less. I moved to Chicago for school about a year after getting clean, and as a way of giving back to the addiction community, began delivering food to the Lower Wacker community and offering recovery resources for any who were interested (which pretty much everyone was).  Never did I feel unsafe in any way.  These people are down on their luck, many of them suffering from addiction and other forms of devastating psychological illness.  They aren't scary, and people spreading misinformation about them being dangerous and to stay away from them is doing nothing to help the problem.  Remember to be respectful - their area is their home, and walking straight into a person's home without asking is not okay, and these people are in no way "less than" because they have found themselves in a desperate situation.  The right (well, wrong) set of circumstances is the only thing between them and yourself - they didn't ask or plan to be in this situation.  Thank you for representing them in a human light, rather than the monstrous image people spread.

A lot of interesting theories here, everyone is blaming the SUV, and I understand why. However, as a first responder, I must share this. Yes, there are laws in all 50 states which require drivers to yield the right of way to responding emergency vehicles, however, when an emergency vehicle has to proceed through a red light, the driver must do so with extreme caution because traffic with the green has some right of way. The reality is the ambulance driver in this situation crossed this intersection a bit too quickly. Most departments require a full stop at a red to first verify that traffic with the green is yielding before proceeding on red, they didn’t which means the ambulance driver is mostly responsible for the accident. Emergency services must respond with due regard for public safety.
